query: definition of pyrexia of unknown origin

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fever of unknown origin (FUO) was first described by Dr. Petersdorf and Dr. Beesom in 1961. [1] FUO was defined as a temperature of 101 degrees Fahrenheit (38.3 degrees Centigrade) or higher with a minimum duration of three weeks without an established diagnosis despite at least one week's investigation in the hospital. This definition was later changed to accommodate technological advances allowing for sophisticated outpatient evaluations, increasing numbers of immunocompromised individuals including those with human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)/acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS), and more complex treatment options becoming available. The revised definition proposed by Durack and Street in 1991 divided cases into four distinct subclasses: classic FUO, nosocomial FUO, neutropenic FUO, and HIV-related FUO. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Fever in the Intensive Care Patient -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The definition of fever in the ICU is different from the usual definition of fever. Fever in an ICU patient is defined as a single temperature of ≥101 degrees F or ≥38.3 C, as per the American College of Critical Care Medicine (ACCCM) and Infectious Disease Society of America (IDSA) joint task force. [1] Also, the ACCCM and IDSA recommend that fever in ICU should be investigated only if the temperature is ≥101 degrees F. Hyperpyrexia or hyperthermia is a temperature of more than 105.8 degrees F (or 41 degrees C) and is uncommonly encountered in intensive care settings.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Evaluation -- Nuclear Medicine Tests
retrieved_docs.content: In the past, nuclear medicine testing was generally reserved for cases that remain undiagnosed after thorough initial evaluation. Recent European studies suggest utilizing fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography (FDG-PET)/CT scan earlier in FUO workup, if available. [10] [11] [12] FDG-PET/CT and FDG/PET are highly sensitive and non-invasive diagnostic techniques for anatomic localization of infectious, inflammatory, or neoplastic processes and, although nonspecific, can guide further definitive tests such as biopsy or aspiration.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to note that immunocompromised and HIV patients may require an entirely different approach in diagnosing and treatment of recurrent fevers. This article focuses on FUO in immunocompetent adult patients. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Evaluation -- Nuclear Medicine Tests
retrieved_docs.content: If FDG-PET is not available, labeled leukocyte studies could be used as an alternative; however, they might have a lower diagnostic yield. Gallium- and indium-labeled leukocyte studies are highly sensitive but not specific enough for establishing a diagnosis. However, these tests are helpful to localize the involved site for a targeted evaluation with a CT scan. Note that indium scans have a high rate of false negatives with bone infections. Positron emission tomography can aid in detecting obscure infections or malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the workup of a patient with a fever of unknown origin. Outline the causes for fever of unknown origin. Summarize the treatment of patients with fever of unknown origin. Review the importance of improving care coordination among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes for patients affected by fever of unknown origin. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Fever of unknown origin (FUO) was first described by Dr. Petersdorf and Dr. Beesom in 1961. FUO was defined as a temperature of 101 degrees Fahrenheit (38.3 degrees Centigrade) or higher with a minimum duration of three weeks without an established diagnosis after an intensive one-week investigation in the hospital. Today, due to technological advances allowing for sophisticated outpatient evaluations, the one-week inpatient investigation is no longer required. This activity reviews the cause and presentation of fever of unknown origin and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When working up the differential diagnosis for FUO, it is important to remember that the cause is more likely a subtle or atypical manifestation of a common disease rather than a rare disease. Diagnosing a cause of FUO can be a cumbersome task and requires repeated diligent and thorough history taking along with a complete physical examination.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Fever -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: It is essential to understand that the definition of fever is not the same as that of hyperthermia (hyperpyrexia). In fever, there is an increase in the 'set-point' temperature brought about by the hypothalamus, enabling the body to maintain a controlled increase in the core temperature and general functionality of all organ systems. In hyperthermia, however, the rise in the body's core temperature is beyond the confines of the set-point temperature and regulation of the hypothalamus.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: There is no clear-cut diagnostic approach to fever of unknown origin (FUO). Thorough history with a focus on the most probable etiology based on the patient’s symptoms is the key to pinpoint the origin of FUO. Information about previous illnesses, localizing symptoms, alcohol intake, home medications, occupational exposures, pets, travel, and familial disorders should not be overlooked. Constellation of patient-reported symptoms should help providers narrow down the etiology of the etiologic category of fevers as each of these has clinical hallmarks. For example, if a patient presents with B-symptoms, early satiety, and significant weight loss, the provider should pursue a malignancy workup. On the other hand, if a patient presents with rigors, an infectious etiology should be considered, while joint involvement is a hallmark of rheumatologic disorders. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: It is important to remember that up to 51% of cases remain undiagnosed. However, the prognosis for these patients is generally good, and it is highly probable that FUO will spontaneously resolve in weeks to months. In stable patients without a diagnosis, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs could be used for symptomatic management. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Evaluation -- Non-invasive Tests
retrieved_docs.content: To diagnose FUO, the non-invasive testing outlined above should have been inconclusive. At this point, a clinician should exclude surreptitious manipulation of the thermometer and analyze patients' medication lists to evaluate for drug-induced fevers. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Algor Mortis -- Definition/Introduction -- Mechanisms
retrieved_docs.content: Throughout life, the hypothalamus regulates body temperature to maintain an internal temperature of approximately 36.9 °C (98.6 °F). Heat dissipation results from the body's inability to maintain this equilibrium after death. Due to residual metabolic activity, the cooling rate initially slows before gradually following a sigmoid curve. The core temperature stays constant for several hours before dropping steadily over the next 12 hours, often at a pace of 1 to 1.5 °C/h. However, environmental factors have a significant impact on this rate. Notably, ambient conditions such as humidity, air circulation, and closeness to heat sources can significantly impact the cooling rate.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Etiology -- Common Causes of Fever in the Different Subclasses
retrieved_docs.content: Nosocomial FUO: Healthcare-associated fevers can be due to drug fever, complications post-operatively, venous thromboembolic disease, malignancy, transfusion-related reactions, or Clostridium difficile infection. [7] Risk factors such as surgical procedures, instrumentation, intravascular devices, immobilization, and medications can help determine the diagnostic testing necessary to obtain a diagnosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Fever of Unknown Origin -- Etiology -- Common Causes of Fever in the Different Subclasses
retrieved_docs.content: HIV-related FUO: Fevers can be present during acute illness, but are also common in the setting of untreated infection signifying additional infection with opportunistic organisms. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1810
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “Above reference range” is interpreted as “الحُمَّى” and is described as an increased “isa”. In the classification of febrile disorders, the entity “fever of unknown origin” includes “الحُمَّى” as a child concept. Therapeutically, the drug salsalate may be used to treat “الحُمَّى”. Within symptom coding, the identifier “ssc” is linked to the complaint “ألم في العنق” when it occurs in the setting of “الحُمَّى”. Clinically, “الحُمَّى” is associated with the cardiac rhythm disturbance “رفرفة أذينية”. Finally, the broader “Symptoms and Complaints Component” is organized with “الحُمَّى” as its parent category, reflecting the central role of fever in the presentation of related signs and symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 366
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are described as closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this category, malaise and fatigue is identified as a specific parent symptom cluster that belongs to the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. The concept of malaise and fatigue is mapped to the Arabic terms الشعور بالضعف and النوام, and is also associated with the alternative Arabic expression الضعف العصبي. This mapping highlights the cross‑linguistic equivalence of malaise and fatigue with these Arabic descriptors, reinforcing its role as a key manifestation within ill‑defined clinical presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 998
retrieved_docs.content: Malaise and fatigue, which is mapped to the Arabic expressions الشعور بالضعف and النوام and also associated with الضعف العصبي, serves as a parent category for the broader grouping termed signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions. Within this grouping, the concept غشي is identified as a child element. Additionally, the term Symptoms is described as related and possibly synonymous with signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions, indicating overlap in clinical description. Understanding these hierarchical and synonymous relationships helps clinicians recognize that malaise and fatigue and its Arabic equivalents encompass a range of nonspecific signs, including غشي, and that the label Symptoms may be used interchangeably with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1488
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely linked to, and may be considered synonymous with, the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this category, the specific manifestation of abdominal pain (ألم بطني) is identified as a child symptom, illustrating how particular clinical presentations fall under the umbrella of ill‑defined condition signs and symptoms. This relationship underscores the importance of recognizing individual symptoms such as abdominal pain as part of a broader, sometimes nonspecific, clinical picture.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2143
retrieved_docs.content: Fever (الحُمَّى) is a central clinical concept that integrates several related conditions and symptoms. Fever of unknown origin is classified as a specific subtype under the broader category of fever, indicating that it is a child concept of الحُمَّى. The non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug salsalate may be used to treat fever, reflecting a therapeutic relationship between salsalate and الحُمَّى. Within the Symptoms and Complaints Component, fever is identified as a parent category, showing that many patient‑reported complaints are organized under الحُمَّى. Clinically, fever is associated with inflammatory joint diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (التهاب المفاصل الروماتيودي) and gouty arthritis (التهاب المفاصل المعدي), underscoring its frequent occurrence in these disorders. Additionally, the ssc entity links fever with neck pain (ألم في العنق), highlighting that cervical discomfort can accompany febrile episodes. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand the role of fever across diverse disease presentations and guides appropriate management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1209
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are considered related and possibly synonymous with signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions. The category signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions includes specific manifestations such as ألم بطني (abdominal pain) and malaise and fatigue. Abdominal pain is identified as a child of signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions and is clinically associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes). Malaise and fatigue is also a child of signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions; it is mapped to the term النوام and additionally linked to الضعف العصبي. This network of relationships highlights how general symptom terminology connects to more specific clinical signs, underlying pancreatic pathology, and culturally specific descriptors of fatigue and weakness, aiding comprehensive patient assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2782
retrieved_docs.content: Fever of unknown origin is a clinical syndrome that includes الحُمَّى (fever) as a component. الحُمَّى is clinically associated with التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي (rheumatoid arthritis) and التهاب المفاصل المعدي (gouty arthritis), indicating that fever can be a manifestation of these inflammatory joint diseases. In addition, الحُمَّى is clinically associated with تعاطي التَبغ (tobacco use), suggesting that smoking may contribute to the occurrence or severity of fever. These interconnections underscore the importance of evaluating inflammatory arthritides and smoking history when assessing patients with unexplained fever.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2470
retrieved_docs.content: Fever (الحُمَّى) is a central clinical finding that is linked to several related concepts. It is classified as a child of “fever of unknown origin,” indicating that unexplained prolonged fever falls under the broader category of الحُمَّى. The non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug ibuprofen lysine may be used to treat الحُمَّى, providing symptomatic relief. The ssc component connects neck pain (ألم في العنق) with الحُمَّى, reflecting that cervical discomfort can accompany febrile states. Clinically, الحُمَّى is associated with both rheumatoid arthritis (التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي) and infectious arthritis (التهاب المفاصل المعدي), underscoring its relevance in inflammatory joint diseases. Finally, الحُمَّى serves as a parent category for the Symptoms and Complaints Component, positioning fever as a primary symptom from which other complaints are organized.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2820
retrieved_docs.content: Fever (الحُمَّى) is a central clinical sign that connects multiple disease entities and patient factors. In the taxonomy of febrile illnesses, “fever of unknown origin” is a broader category that includes fever (الحُمَّى) as a child manifestation, while the Symptoms and Complaints Component is organized under fever (الحُمَّى) as its parent, indicating that fever is a primary symptom within that component. Clinically, fever (الحُمَّى) is associated with inflammatory joint diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis (التهاب المفاصل الروماتويدي) and gouty arthritis (التهاب المفاصل المعدي), reflecting that these arthritides often present with febrile episodes. Additionally, tobacco use (تعاطي التَبغ) is also clinically linked to fever (الحُمَّى), suggesting that smoking may contribute to or exacerbate febrile responses. Recognizing these interrelationships helps clinicians evaluate fever in the context of underlying rheumatologic conditions and lifestyle factors, thereby guiding more comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1242
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. Within this group of nonspecific clinical manifestations, the specific finding of الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse) is recognized as a child element, illustrating that severe cardiovascular failure can be one of the many possible presentations encompassed by ill‑defined symptom complexes. This relationship underscores the importance of viewing individual symptoms as part of a larger, sometimes vague, clinical picture when assessing patients with ambiguous or poorly characterized disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 584
retrieved_docs.content: Signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions constitute a broad, overarching category that links together general symptoms, various diseases and injuries, and specific symptom clusters such as malaise and fatigue. The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with this category, emphasizing that many nonspecific clinical presentations fall under the same conceptual umbrella. Within this framework, malaise and fatigue is identified as a child of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions and is mapped to the Arabic expressions “الشعور بالضعف” (feeling of weakness) and “النوام,” as well as to the related term “الضعف العصبي” (neurological weakness). Additionally, the condition “الانهيار في الدورة الدموية” (circulatory collapse) is listed as a child of the same category, illustrating that both mild, vague complaints and severe physiological failures are organized under the same ill‑defined signs and symptoms taxonomy. This hierarchical and multilingual mapping aids clinicians in recognizing how non‑specific complaints like malaise and fatigue relate to broader diagnostic categories and more serious manifestations, supporting comprehensive assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: The medical taxonomy places “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” as a distinct category that includes the specific manifestation labeled “غشي.” The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating substantial overlap in meaning. Moreover, the broader grouping “diseases and injuries” is identified as the parent of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” positioning these vague clinical signs and symptoms as subcomponents within the larger domain of diseases and injuries. This hierarchical and synonymous relationship helps clinicians organize and interpret poorly defined clinical presentations within the overall framework of disease and injury.
